data.title: Silver Thursday Revisited And A Look At SILJ
data.description:
Silver soared above $65 per ounce for the first time at the week’s high, sharply eclipsing the 1980 peak. Volatility has soared with the precious metal, and today I’m taking a step back in time to revisit so-called "Silver Thursday.”

In short, the episode featured the Hunt Brothers’ massive accumulated silver position. Their attempt to corner the silver futures market occurred more than 45 years ago, but with silver now up 115% on the year, the time is right for a history lesson.

I’ll also take a fresh look at the Amplify Junior Silver Miners ETF (SILJ), a fund on which I had a "Buy" rating back in August. I am downgrading the product to a "Hold," given euphoric price action and the real threat of a sharp pullback. But first, let’s profile the Hunt Brothers and their incredible silver saga.

Silver Since 1970

What Is Silver Thursday?

Silver Thursday is an infamous day in finance history. The term refers to when Nelson Bunker Hunt, William Herbert Hunt, and Lamar Hunt aimed to corner the silver futures market. Over time, spot silver and silver futures soared from under $5 per ounce in 1978 to near $50 by January 1980.

The three men, heirs to an oil fortune, built highly leveraged positions in the silver futures market. At one point, the Hunt Brothers’ silver stake was reportedly more than 30% of the world’s supply of privately held silver. Their position was in both physical silver and silver futures contracts, making it an increasingly difficult trade to manage. As time passed, the Hunts stockpiled the metal and continued buying with cash from their inheritance.

As money poured into the silver market, the price naturally soared from the mid-single digits to eventually above $40 per ounce. Amid the rally, the Hunt Brothers were too much for contrarian traders to handle, resulting in a short squeeze of historical proportions.

Trade Lesson: A short squeeze occurs when a large short position exists, often from producers hedging or speculators expecting lower prices, and rising prices force shorts to cover or face margin calls.

After procuring a commanding majority stake in the entire Comex silver futures market, their more than $4 billion position entailed just $1 billion of equity. With the spot price going parabolic, the public began to take notice. Individual investors and just everyday folks began sifting through their possessions to find any silver they could, then pawning them for profits. It was such a mainstream affair that retailer Tiffany & Co. took out a full-page ad in the New York Times to call out the Hunt Brothers’ actions.

Who were the Hunt Brothers?

The Hunt brothers were sons of Texas oil billionaire Haroldson Lafayette Hunt Jr. Upon his passing in 1974, Lafayette Hunt Jr.’s sons took their newfound fortune and invested in a handful of commodities. You see, the three heirs firmly believed in the debasement of the U.S. dollar (something many macro pundits may hold fast to in modern times). Recall that the mid-1970s through the early 1980s were marked by high inflation at home, with a falling dollar value relative to other currencies and bull markets in commodities (like oil and gold).

Precious metals indeed were among the standouts as the 70s played out, despite gold ownership being somewhat restricted in the United States at the time. The Hunts turned to silver for a hedge (and, later, intense speculation). Their accumulation did not happen overnight, though, as they built a 42-million-ounce physical position over more than six years. Their stake turned incredibly lucrative, with silver increasing 10-fold.

What Happened on Silver Thursday?

Pops and drops happen all the time in markets, and silver was a prime case in point in 1979 and early 1980. The ultimate collapse began on Jan. 7, 1980, when the Comex exchange adopted “Silver Rule 7,” which restricted positions through margin requirements on silver futures. The limit on silver futures exposure was set at 3 million ounces, and traders above that threshold had until the next month to comply. The Chicago Board of Trade also suspended the issuance of new silver contracts.

Mechanically, the Hunts’ leveraged position was weakened as lenders could no longer extend the same level of credit. Silver prices started to plunge. Once prices fell far enough, their positions breached minimum margin requirements, triggering massive margin calls.

The scramble to meet these calls culminated in Silver Thursday on March 27, 1980. Silver futures collapsed by more than 33% after the Hunt Brothers failed to meet a $100 million margin call from the Bache brokerage firm. Facing default, Bache began liquidating silver futures.

The rest was history, as spot silver dropped from the low $20s to below $10 (declining 50% over a few sessions), eventually sporting a $4-handle by 1982. The gold-silver ratio (a common precious metal gauge) fell to near 15 at silver’s peak, then soared above 100 a decade later. Today, the ratio is 67, down from 100-plus earlier this year (due to silver’s incredible 115% YTD rise).

Gold:Silver Ratio Falling Fast, Given the Silver Rally

Silver Returns by Year: 2025 Second Only to the Hunt Brothers' 1979 Rally

Impacts of Silver Thursday

Facing systemic risk, the Hunt Brothers received a bailout from a consortium of banks and companies totaling more than $1 billion. The lifeline helped cover obligations to Bache, which survived the crisis. The SEC launched an investigation into the brothers’ attempt to corner the market, and Congress even called them in for questioning. The trio were ultimately charged with manipulation.

The Hunts declared bankruptcy after being fined $134 million. Once among the wealthiest men in America, they were banned from trading commodities.

It took nearly a decade for the Hunts to unwind their silver positions and repay creditors. Silver Thursday remains a textbook example of failed risk management by traders, exchanges, and regulators. Fear and greed often drive markets... look no further than Silver Thursday and the Hunt Brothers.

A Cautionary Tale About Margin Trading

Silver Thursday underscores the importance of risk management in leveraged trading. If your position grows too large relative to your equity, small price moves can lead to ruin. That’s as true for major players like the Hunt Brothers decades ago as it is for individual investors today. Traders should always monitor their leverage and consider downside scenarios, especially during volatile markets.

At the moment, silver’s implied volatility is high at 44%. Take caution.

Silver ETF (SLV) Implied Volatility High Near 45%

ETF Profile: SILJ

Now let’s bring risk management to today by taking a fresh look at SILJ, the junior silver miner equity ETF I profiled in the summer. You can review the fundamentals, including my take on the valuation and seasonal trends, in that previous piece.

For the chart, notice in the graph below that shares put in a notable bearish reversal pattern on Friday, Dec. 12. In my view, it’s key for the fund to sustain above the October peak of $27.26, particularly on a weekly basis. I see support at the November rebound high and subsequent December low just below the $25 mark. Below that, the November low of $20.46 is a potential target if we see a protracted pullback.

With high volatility, we can’t rule out severe downside moves. But I would be surprised to see SILJ fall to where the long-term 200-day moving average is right now—close to $17. That’s where the ETF really began to take flight in Q3.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the chart. It printed a bearish divergence to price, as the RSI failed to make a new high while price did. That’s a bearish sign.

Overall, SILJ is at risk of falling back into the $20 to $27 range.

SILJ: Bearish Reversal Risk, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

With silver on most active investors’ radars, it’s high time for a history lesson. Silver Thursday and the story of the Hunt Brothers is an epic one in the world of investing. It’s a reminder that risk happens fast, and with a parabolic run-up in silver the commodity, there are some signs that shares of silver mining companies could consolidate after an incredible year.

data.title: CoreWeave: This Is What Real AI Scale Looks Like
data.description:
Introduction

AI data center stocks, of which the most prominent are CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV), Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS), and IREN Limited (IREN), have come under a lot of pressure in recent weeks, losing 53%, 33%, and 42% off their highs, respectively, while the S&P 500 index (SPX) is inching closer to new all-time highs. It is not unreasonable to say that investors who bought the high have been getting a bit burnt, at least for now.

Sector-wide, part of the sell-off in these names has been a result of de-risking (as these companies are still cash-burning and high beta in line with a slight broader market pullback) and profit-taking (after having rallied multiple hundreds of percent from the April lows).

Previously, I rated IREN a Strong Buy on November 18 and NBIS a Strong Buy on November 25. I have not yet covered CoreWeave, which is due for change.

Regarding CRWV, there are a few company-intrinsic issues that led to a larger selloff than the two aforementioned names, as, unlike the other two names, the stock has not surpassed its June highs in October. They had a delay in bringing capacity online with one of their providers. However, the customer agreed to extend the contract, which only delays revenue recognition.

This led to their FY25 outlook being revised and not matching the market's high expectations, though still promising rapid growth moving forward. The updated numbers stand as follows:

  • Revenue: $5.05-5.15B
  • Adjusted Operating Income: $690-720M
  • Interest Expense: $1.21-1.25B
  • Capital Expenditures: $12-14B

Previously trading at a Price/Gross profit north of 40, CoreWeave was priced to perfection, certainly leaving no room for errors like these. Naturally, this tied into the stock's decline as well.

All of this crippled investor sentiment, which in my view is a buying opportunity, as despite headwinds such as high debt and no near-term profitability, CoreWeave is a critical infrastructure provider, set to close many more crucial contracts and trades at a cheap valuation with a large revenue backlog.

Making sense of the opportunity

Compute demand growth acts as a significant tailwind for AI infrastructure providers. The global AI data center GPU market alone is projected to grow from about $10.5 billion in 2025 to roughly $77 billion by 2035, implying sustained multiyear expansion in demand for high-performance AI hardware and the facilities that host it. This corresponds to a long-term growth trajectory of roughly 22 percent compounded annually over the next decade.

Companies operating in this space portray this “hunger,” as Oracle (ORCL), Nebius, Microsoft's Azure (MSFT), Google Cloud (GOOG), and Amazon's AWS (AMZN) are all operating at or beyond their available AI GPU capacity, as confirmed by their latest quarterly results and commentary. Demand is therefore not the limiting factor, at least for now.

Further, CoreWeave scored the platinum rating from SemiAnalysis' rating of neoclouds twice in a row, which no one else has won once:

The Platinum ClusterMAX™ tier represents the highest level of recognition in the AI cloud market. According to SemiAnalysis, providers at this level “consistently excel across all evaluation criteria,” including robust security, competitive pricing, extensive technical expertise, outstanding reliability, and a seamlessly managed Slurm and Kubernetes offering.

In my view, this makes it highly likely that CRWV will be able to close further major deals and continue to aggressively grow their backlog as customers need reliability, scale, and competitive pricing.

Speaking of deals, CoreWeave recently announced a new agreement with Runway, under which Runway will use CoreWeave’s Nvidia GB300 NVL72 clusters and integrated Weights and Biases tooling to train and serve its next-generation AI video models. Financial terms were not disclosed, but it is a contract that still matters strategically because it shows that independent AI application companies are choosing CoreWeave.

CoreWeave’s growth is funded by heavy leverage and long-term leases rather than owned data centers. As of September 30, the company had $14.2B in principal debt, including term loans at 9–15%, 10% senior notes (2030/2031), a 7% revolver, 8–10% OEM financing, and a 12% Magnetar loan. Repayments are front-loaded: $0.6B is due in the rest of 2025, $3.9B in 2026, and $3.0B in 2027. Interest already weighs heavily, as Q3 net interest expense was $311M on $1.37B of revenue, and year-to-date interest reached $841M on $3.56B, roughly one-quarter of revenue. CRWV recently completed a $2.25B convertible senior notes offering due 2031 with a 1.75% coupon. This deal lowers CRWV's cash interest burden compared to its double-digit-rate debt but adds potential dilution.

Valuation

Compared to NBIS and IREN, the only other providers that secured multiple Big Tech contracts, CoreWeave trades at a significant discount based on their P/S multiple of 8.5.

Of course, high amounts of debt and a more mature business (they reported $1.4B of quarterly revenue, whereas comps AI revenues are in the low millions and are priced for more explosive growth) need to be somewhat discounted. But even looking at their CY2026 P/S multiple, they are significantly less expensive than said two peers, at:

  • CRWV 3.6
  • IREN 4.6
  • NBIS 6.8

Therefore, I do see some mean reversion as a likely outcome moving forward, as soon as sentiment around this stock improves. A major catalyst could be further large contracts signed.

Still, current valuations seem to price in no further backlog expansion and an incredibly long path to profitability, if any at all. In my view, this is too pessimistic, and CRWV will be able to outperform these expectations, making some mean reversion here likely. Even if CoreWeave retraces to their average P/S of around 15, that would bring in almost 50% of gains immediately, and even more with rising sales along with future reported quarterly results.

Risks

CoreWeave is exposed to macro risk because it is high beta, unprofitable, and highly leveraged. Its valuation is sensitive to interest rates and broader market sentiment. Interest expense already consumes roughly one quarter of revenue, which reduces financial flexibility if capital becomes more expensive or risk appetite declines.

Execution risk is the most significant. The company must bring leased data center capacity online on schedule to convert its $55.6B backlog into revenue. The above-mentioned delay and the market reaction from that show that there is no room for such error.

Customer concentration is also a central risk. CoreWeave is more reliant on OpenAI than peers NBIS and IREN. OpenAI accounts for roughly 70% of CoreWeave’s year-to-date revenue, yet OpenAI’s own financial profile shows a large gap between revenue and spending. OpenAI generated about $4.3B in revenue in the first half of 2025, yet external analysis indicates that OpenAI has committed to roughly $1T in long-term compute and infrastructure spending over the next decade, a level that far exceeds its current annual revenue. They must continue growing usage and maintain competitiveness against Gemini, Claude, and others to support these commitments.

Conclusion

Today, CoreWeave seems worth a bet as a part of an AI data center exposure in a diversified portfolio. I would not recommend more than 5% of portfolio allocation into CRWV. When the valuation becomes too stretched again, CRWV would turn into a Hold/Sell quickly, as this is mainly a multiples mean reversion play for me, as fundamentally, I favor Iren and Nebius, but that is subject to developments in macro/AI data center markets and CRWV's future financial reporting and commentary.

Rating: Initiate Buy.

data.title: Dow Year-End Rally: Top Dividend And Value ETFs For A 2026 Value Rotation
data.description:
Value and dividend ETFs for an expensive market

The Dow Jones Industrial Average, or DJIA (DJI), is considered the wiser value index of the 3 major indexes, or at least that's what it was once thought of. With that in mind, any time there is a value rotation, the Dow tends to outperform the S&P 500 (SP500) for stints.

The perception of value

As we'll see in a bit, the Dow is still expensive, almost just as much so as the S&P 500, but the perception of value remains. There are better alternatives out there if one is so inclined to rotate to value.

With the S&P 500 trading with a FWD P/E in excess of 22 X on the S&P 500 (SP500), there's little question that the major market indexes (SPY, QQQ, DIA, VOO, IVV) are trading at elevated levels heading into 2026. Investors may be looking to improve the overall P/E ratio of their portfolio and diversify away from the information technology sector. To be more specific, here are the major indexes estimated FWD P/E data:

Index Forward P/E (Estimate)- denoted by ^
Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) 22.74×
Dow Jones Transportation Average 22.70×
Dow Jones Utility Average 25.35×
Russell 2000 Index 22.65×
NASDAQ-100 Index 26.25×
S&P 500 Index 22.21×

Interestingly enough via data from Barron's, the DJIA has a higher forward multiple at current than the S&P 500. This is most likely due to both the recent rally and the lower forward estimation of earnings growth. This cost weighted index [rather than market cap], still has several slow growers as the top weightings as can be seen here:

The forward market multiple and earnings yields

With every major index [including small caps] trading at multiples in excess of 22 X, let's put that in perspective of an earnings yield by inverting the P/E ratio, dividing 1 by the ratio will get us the yield.

Index Forward P/E Earnings Yield
Dow Jones Industrial Average 22.74× 4.40%
Dow Jones Transportation Average 22.70× 4.41%
Dow Jones Utility Average 25.35× 3.94%
Russell 2000 Index 22.65× 4.42%
NASDAQ-100 Index 26.25× 3.81%
S&P 500 Index 22.21× 4.50%

When looking at what we're paying for the indexes from this perspective, it's interesting to compare that earnings yield to the various "risk free" rates:

We can then reverse this again for a visualization of what each maturity would be as an equivalent P/E ratio:

Maturity Yield P/E equivalency
US 3-Month 3.67% 27.25×
US 12-Month 3.56% 28.09×
US 2-Year 3.54% 28.25×
US 5-Year 3.72% 26.88×
US 10-Year 4.14% 24.15×
US 30-Year 4.78% 20.92×

10 year treasury "risk free" trends

Right now the US 10 yr (US10Y) seems to be in a holding pattern to slightly rising. A lot of this is being blamed on Japan and other foreign countries that may be in a hiking cycle in relation to the U.S. cutting cycle. Whether or not a new FED chair and revamped board of governors next year will be able to get this under control is yet to be seen. If the "risk free" rate used for discounting equities and building loan rate profiles doesn't sink below 4%, then all the major indexes may be questionably valued even with strong growth rates in earnings.

If you were to take a blended average of all the major indexes, they currently produce an average forward earnings yield of 4.28%. The US 10 year is now back up to 4.15-4.2%. This is not a great spread above the risk free rate.

Our ETF comparisons today

Ticker Fund Name Div. Yield % P/E Earnings Yield % (Correct) 5-yr DGR % Score
(PVAL) Putnam Focused Large Cap Value 1.12 16.42 6.09 30.0* 37.21
(SCHD) Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity 3.78 16.68 5.99 10.38 20.15
(FDVV) Fidelity High Dividend 3.03 20.29 4.93 10.42 18.38
(DHS) WisdomTree U.S. High Dividend 3.18 16.50 6.06 3.99 13.23
(VYM) Vanguard High Dividend Yield 2.54 19.80 5.05 4.11 11.70

There are other index funds and value strategy ETFs in addition to just defaulting to the DJIA, small, mid or equal weight funds. Above are some that are on my menu of value rotation plays where I personally put funds into or would consider putting funds into if I wanted to lower my overall portfolio P/E ratio. These 5 funds listed above all have earnings yields nearly 1-2% above the US 10 year that should grow, versus being of the static risk-free variety.

I scored them using a factor based system, this system ranked these funds by adding earnings yield to the 5 year dividend growth rate. Earnings yield of the fund represents "cheapness" and the dividend growth represents that basket of stock's ability to grow yield and ultimately the underlying earnings. While holdings in all of these funds or strategies are subject to change, this is where their valuations lie here in mid December 2025.

Comparing the average payout ratios

Ticker Dividend Yield % Earnings Yield % Payout Ratio (DY ÷ EY) Interpretation
PVAL 1.12 6.09 0.18 (18.4%) conservative payout
SCHD 3.78 5.99 0.63 (63.1%) Moderately high payout
FDVV 3.03 4.93 0.61 (61.5%) Moderately high payout
DHS 3.18 6.06 0.53 (52.5%) More conservative
VYM 2.54 5.05 0.50 (50.3%) More conservative

Also nice to compare is the current payout ratio of the earnings yield in relation to the dividend yield. The lower the ratio, the more earnings retention for the portfolio and the higher the future prospects for dividend growth. PVAL is the absolute lowest here, but we can also expect a higher fluctuation in the underlying holdings due to the active management nature of the fund.

Of the passive index funds, VYM has the most conservative payout ratio of earnings which could help with the index fund's dividend growth rate in years to come. SCHD has the highest relative payout ratio.

Performance ex PVAL

Since PVAL, an active value ETF, does not yet have a decade of data to backtest, we'll first look at the total returns of all these value funds in this article that have existed for that length of time [10 yrs+]. FDVV has had the best 10 year record with a higher allocation to growth and tech. VYM and SCHD sit in the middle of the pack with DHS coming in last over this period.

Performance for duration of PVAL existence

Now including PVAL in this mix, which again does not yet have a decade of data to backtest, shows that it is superior to all of these funds in total return even when charging a higher expense ratio. Right now, active management in this fund is proving superior to passive investing in value.

SCHD strategy

The stocks in this popular index fund need to have paid dividends for at least 10 years, have a fair market cap of at least $500 million and have an average daily trading volume of at least $2 million. These stocks are then ordered by indicated annual dividend [FWD yield].

The index only takes into account the top half of the stocks that pass the first screen.

Then the stocks are ranked by 4 fundamental values:

  • Free cash flow to total debt.
  • Return on equity.
  • FWD yield.
  • 5 year dividend growth rate.

Stocks are re-weighted quarterly, and no single holding can represent more than 4% of the portfolio. The 4% weighting is interesting as it's similar to money market safety rules where no single issue can exceed 5% of total holdings.

This index fund has one of the lowest expense ratios of all value dividend index funds at .06%. This fund seeks to replicate the Dow Dividend 100 index.

VYM strategy

VYM strategy from Vanguard investor relations (emphasis added):

-Seeks to track the performance of the FTSE® High Dividend Yield Index, which measures the investment return of common stocks of companies characterized by high dividend yields.

-Provides a convenient way to track the performance of stocks that are forecasted to have above-average dividend yields.

-Follows a passively managed, full-replication approach.

The current actual dividend yield on this is middle of the pack at 2.38%. The current holdings also have popular tech names included, focusing pretty evenly on growth and yield at the same time.

PVAL strategy

I love writing on Seeking Alpha because the site has some of the most astute readers and analysts. Many are wealthy themselves, FIRE or on time retired and have their own fingers on the pulse of the market. This fund was brought to my attention by one of those astute readers and I have added it to my personal menu of funds for my own personal value rotations.

This is an active ETF managed by Putnam, part of Franklin Templeton and uses a proprietary value target strategy in an attempt to produce alpha over the other various value funds. It's a young fund [inception in 2021], but so far the active management has provided value beating most other value funds over the period of time they have existed. The gross expense ratio at .56% is hefty compared to other index funds, but if the fund is outperforming, the higher fees can be warranted. Here is the fund's primary focus:

-Relative value approach. Defines the value universe daily with fundamental and quantitative tools to identify unique value opportunities.

-Focus on cash flows. Puts distinct emphasis on companies able and willing to return cash to shareholders, and focuses on future cash flows instead of earnings to evaluate value creation.

-Disciplined risk management. Utilizes proprietary tools to maximize stock-specific risk and limit unintended factor risks, seeking alpha over a full market cycle.

DHS strategy

From Wisdom Tree Investor Relations (emphasis added):

The fund invests in stocks of companies operating across diversified sectors. It invests in growth and value stocks of companies across diversified market capitalization. It invests in dividend paying stocks of companies. The investment seeks to track the price and yield performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree U.S. High Dividend Index.

This is an interesting strategy being that the company is also the producer of their own proprietary high dividend yield index. The fees here are a little higher than index funds at .38%, but are not quite as high as paying for active management. This fund is extremely diversified at 325 holdings.

FDVV strategy

From Fidelity:

This fund uses the Fidelity High Dividend index as a primary benchmark and the Russel 1000 as a secondary one. Even Though this index fund has a lot of growth stocks in the top holdings, it still manages to produce a current dividend yield of 3%. This is another in-house proprietary index which usually leads to a higher expense ratio, in this case .15%. Still, this is very cheap all things considered for a unique dividend index.

Risks and summary

Lowering your portfolio's overall P/E ratio has it's share of risks. When lowering your P/E ratio you also lower your growth potential if these companies on average retain less income and pay more out as dividend. If the appetite for growth and tech holds for the next year or two, these strategies will most likely underperform.

These funds do however serve as some alternatives to defaulting to the DJIA as the more value oriented index which as of current, it might be more expensive than the S&P 500 due to lower predicted growth prospects. The cost weighting nature rewards companies that do fewer stock splits rather than those the grow market capitalization. For preservation and smoothing out of volatility, value strategies might gain popularity [finally]in 2026.

data.title: SBA Communications: A Reasonably Priced Bet On Wireless
data.description:
SBA Communications (SBAC) is about the lowest it's been in five years. While not heavily discounted, it's a fair price for a solid piece of telecom infrastructure, and it's well positioned if opportunities for more rapid growth present themselves.

Business Model

SBA is, in essence, telecom real estate. They generally own towers and similar sites that enable signal transmission for wireless communication. Most of their revenue comes from the big three telecoms: T-Mobile (TMUS), AT&T (T), and Verizon (VZ).

They also own assets outside of the United States, generally in emerging markets, which are a minority of revenue but still important.

International leases, mostly among their emerging market assets, make up about 25% of revenue. Leases typically last 5-10 years with renewal options and annual escalators.

While REITs are capital-intensive, SBA's cash flow margin, consistently above 80%, shows that it's a business that scales well and that can price its leases effectively.

Financial History

As demand for towers and wireless infrastructure has been in a constant state of growth for several years now, this is seen in SBA's financial results.

Revenue grew from $1,638.5M in 2015 to $2,679.6M in 2024. The slight decline between 2023 and 2024 was caused by foreign currency fluctuations from their international operations.

For the first three quarters of 2025, the normal trend of incremental growth has continued without much issue.

Another important metric that shows SBAC's success over time is Adjusted Funds From Operations ("AFFO") per share, as opposed to straight dividends.

This effectively shows what the cash flow is for each individual share. Note that, while revenue grew modestly, this figure has much more growth.

This is because SBAC did not start distributing dividends until 2019. Prior to that, buybacks were the main source of returns and continue to be part of the capital allocation, albeit less so than dividends now.

An interesting difference is that SBA is much more leveraged, relative to the value of their assets, many of which are intangible. As of Q3, they had $11.9B in long-term debt against $11.3B in assets.

Outlook and Valuation

At $193 per share and a $20B market cap, I think SBAC is fairly valued for a base case but could be a beneficiary of better-than-expected luck.

I valued SBAC with a Discounted Cash Flow model that used AFFO per share (instead of free cash flow). I estimated single-digit growth and a modest terminal multiple, to give us a fair value very close to the current value, using a 10% discount rate (typical return of a market index).

I think there are many scenarios in which an investor will see modest returns over time, with the 5% CAGR supported by buybacks, the escalators in their leases, and their general ability to acquire more towers internationally as more of the world invests in these capabilities.

There will be some near-term deceleration because they sold off their Central American assets, but going forward, AFFO per share should improve as they deleverage. Speaking in the Q3 earnings call, VP of Finance Mark DeRussy noted:

Given the rising rate environment over the past few years and the lack of actionable M&A opportunities at attractive valuation, we have been operating below our steady target leverage of 7 to 7.5x net debt to last quarter annualized EBITDA.

As cash flow is good, it makes sense to takes some of this debt off the balance sheet while they have time to repay. By reducing their interest expense, AFFO per share can grow, and it can improve their credit rating to support M&A at lower cost in the future.

This is to say: I'm not expecting SBAC to take off particularly soon, and its 2.35% dividend yield doesn't make for great income in meantime. Rather, I'm of the view that SBAC has the right assets that lend themselves to compounding over time. If there is an uptick in demand, particularly if long-term rates soften, they will be well-positioned to move on it. I think it's a good vehicle for total returns.

Risks

Obviously, interest rates are the biggest risk. If the Fed begins hiking again as some of these maturities come due years later, that can make refinancing painful. As there aren't a lot of tangible assets either, that increases the risk.

While their concentration on T-Mobile, Verizon, and AT&T is a good source of revenue now, a problem with just one of these customers can be a problem for any growth prediction. I'll remind folks that AT&T's own debt caught up with it, causing it to spin off Warner Bros. (WBD) a few years ago, so occasionally they have to make tough decisions too.

Conclusion

While I don't foresee SBAC being an explosive grower, I see investors getting good value over the long-term at this entry price. Wireless tower infrastructure is an essential at this point, it's difficult to see them struggling without something much worse going on in the world. That's why I initiate coverage today with a Buy rating.

data.title: Oklo's Next Test: Turning Ambition Into Actual Reactors (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I previously suggested that Oklo Inc. (OKLO) was downstream of bottleneck in the nascent SMR nuclear industry. While the ability to supply low-cost HALEU is critical to the success of most SMR designs, Oklo's share price performance suggests that this isn't a concern for investors. I believe that this is a mistake though, and likely stems from hype around the potential of SMRs to meet the energy demands created by AI data centers.

Oklo has also benefitted from a number of positive developments on the regulatory front in recent months. While this is a positive for believers in the technology, Oklo now needs to execute, and there are no excuses for failing to do so.

I believe Oklo will remain a proxy for AI power demand rather than trading on its own fundamentals in the short-term. The large amount of uncertainty around technical feasibility and cost are likely to come to a head over the next few years though.

Market Conditions

The DoE's Reactor Pilot Program is a parallel licensing approach which is expected to accelerate deployment timelines. The program is for R&D and testing purposes rather than commercial operations though. 11 advanced reactor projects were selected to participate in the program, including 3 Oklo projects. While Oklo's reactor design still needs to go through the standard NRC licensing process before it can be used for commercial operations, the DoE has authorized the construction and operation of a nuclear facility at Idaho National Laboratory (Aurora-INL).

There is also the Fuel Line Pilot Program which provides an alternative pathway for fuel fabrication capacity development that should prove faster. This program is complementary to the reactor pilot program and aims to provide the specialized fuels ((HALEU, TRISO)) needed by many advanced reactors.

While these regulatory developments are a positive, Oklo's share price is also heavily dependent on investor sentiment towards AI. Oklo’s 14 GW backlog is primarily made up of data center and hyperscaler customers, although the company's pipeline also encompasses utilities and defense.

AI data centers are the initial focus of most SMR companies due to the expected increase in power demand that will be difficult to meet with other sources. This is supported by the fact that nuclear is a reliable and low carbon power source. The AI data center opportunity alone is expected to be large, with estimates suggesting that something like an additional 50 GW of capacity could be needed. There are signs that OpenAI's (OPENAI) growth is starting to moderate, though, which could become important for company's dependent on the AI CapEx narrative.

It is worth noting that Google AI Overviews launched in May and AI Mode was introduced in June, which is likely at least in part responsible for the slowdown. If this is the case, it would be negative for OpenAI, but shouldn't impact power demand.

The long-term viability of the investments needed to support the continued scale-up of models is likely to come into focus over the next 12 months as well. Many of the companies leading the AI data center buildout are facing free cash flow headwinds, and this situation is likely to get worse before it gets better. Investors have been willing to look past this so far due to the expectation of future growth, but I don't think this situation will persist much longer.

Oklo Business Updates

On the licensing front, Oklo is one of nine companies that has been selected to participate in the DoE's reactor pilot program. Oklo has had 3 projects selected for the program out of a total 11 projects. Oklo has now broken ground at its Aurora-INL site and has commenced procurement activities. Construction and procurement are also underway for the Atomic Alchemy pilot project.

Oklo also received an update from the NRC on its Combined License Application timeline. The company's Principal Design Criteria topical report has been accepted for review under an accelerated timeline, with the NRC indicating that the draft evaluation is expected by early 2026. Oklo also completed a readiness assessment with the NRC for the Phase 1 of its COLA application, which found no gaps to application acceptance for review.

Oklo is also making progress on the testing front, successfully completing a full-scale flow test of a prototype fuel assembly, generating data that can be used to validate simulations. This helps to advance fuel assembly design towards production.

With Oklo now set to start constructing its first reactor, investor focus will now start to shift to the company's ability to execute on its vision. The experience of China and Russia with SMRs could be instructive in this regard, and it isn't particularly reassuring. Russia probably has the most experience with SMRs at the moment, which partly stems from experience operating small reactors in its nuclear-powered icebreaker fleet. The KLT-40S is a 150 MW reactor that uses uranium enriched to the mid to high teens and has a 3 year fuel cycle.

A plant using two of these reactors supplies electricity and heat to remote Arctic-region communities. Initial planning began in 1998, but a site wasn’t selected until 2012. Construction and testing was completed in 2017, with criticality achieved in 2018 and full commissioning in 2020. The KLT-40 design evolved into the RITM-200, which appears to be the focus of future plans. Russia's regulator granted a construction license for a RITM-200N in the Far East of Russia in 2023. This project has a target operation date of 2028.

While Russia continues to pursue SMRs, it is so far only doing so in remote areas where economics are less of a consideration. The country has also fallen well short of prior plans. For example, in 2013 Russia was targeting up to 40 GW SMR installed capacity by 2030.

China's HTR-PM (high-temperature gas-cooled modular pebble bed) reactor demonstrator commenced commercial operations in 2023. Development of the HTR-PM reactor stretches back to the early 90s, although construction of the demonstration plant only commenced in 2012. Civil works were completed in 2016, with the fuel plant reaching its expected production capacity in 2017. Commissioning and testing were completed in 2020 and first criticality was achieved in 2021.

In addition to extended construction timelines and cost overruns, the KLT-40S and HTR-PM reactors have operated at low capacity factors. The HTR-PM had a load factor in the single digits in its first 12 months after start-up and the two KLT-40S reactors have had load factors in the high 20s to load 30s range. If China and Russia's experiences are any guide, it could be upwards of 5 years before Oklo is producing electricity, and even then utilization could be low.

On the fuel front, Oklo recently announced an advanced fuel center in Tennessee, the first phase of which will be a fuel recycling facility. A licensing project plan for the facility has been completed with the NRC, although production isn't expected to start until the early 2030s. Oklo is also exploring recycling the Tennessee Valley Authority's used fuel at the site and evaluating power sales from future Oklo sites to the TVA.

Oklo has also been selected to participate in the DoE's Fuel Line Pilot Program, which aims to support the domestication of advanced nuclear reactor fuel supply chains. As part of the program, Oklo will build and operate 3 facilities. The DoE has approved the Nuclear Safety Design Agreement for the Aurora Fuel Fabrication Facility at Idaho National Laboratory. This site will fabricate fuel for Oklo's first commercial-scale reactor.

Recycling could improve the economics of Oklo's reactors and should help to build domestic supply chains, supported by the fact that there is more than 94,000 metric tons of used nuclear fuel stored at facilities across the U.S. While this part of Oklo's business holds promise, there is uncertainty, and it is worthwhile discussing why recycling has been limited in the past, and why the time could be ripe for change.

Recycling extracts additional energy from fuel and reduces the volume and radioactivity of waste. While reprocessing occurs in France, Russia, Japan, the UK and India, capacity is modest. This likely stems from the fact that the economics are currently unfavorable and that there are proliferation concerns with the most commonly used process.

The main process at the moment is PUREX, which is chemically complex and involves radioactive nuclides, making it expensive. Cost estimates are in the $585-1300 USD per kilogram range and generally suggest a once-through fuel cycle is preferable to reprocessing. MOX fuel fabrication is also required, which adds to the cost of reprocessing fuel considerably due primarily to the fact that plutonium is being handled. While reprocessing can also reduce waste disposal and storage costs, the benefit isn't that large, as storage cost isn't really dictated by volume.

Oklo plans on utilizing an electrochemical process, which could have more attractive economics. In pyroprocessing, actinide anions (including uranium and plutonium) are recovered together, reducing proliferation concerns. Oklo has successfully demonstrated its fuel recycling process in collaboration with Argonne National Laboratory and Idaho National Laboratory. This is notable because Argonne National Laboratory utilized pyroprocessing for used fuel from the EBR-II experimental fast reactor.

Cost estimates for pyroprocessing range from $781 USD per kg to $2,600 USD per kg. Operations and maintenance dominate total costs and pyroprocessing is feasible at a much smaller scale, dramatically reducing CapEx requirements. Fuel fabrication costs are in addition to this, bringing the total cost into the $2,000-7,000 USD per kg range. Oklo has also suggested that recycling could reduce its fuel costs by up to 80%, which broadly tracks with external estimates.

Government support could be considered somewhat perplexing given that the economics of recycling are not really clear. It could be as much about about a desire for energy independence as economics though, as the U.S. imports the vast majority of its uranium needs. While domestic uranium production could be increased significantly, it will likely never be sufficient to meet demand, particularly if a nuclear renaissance occurs.

Reprocessing isn't necessarily make or break for Oklo though, as the company has significant flexibility in terms of fuel ((HALEU, recycled material and down-blended alternatives)). The DoE will provide fuel in the short-term from sources like EBR-II (5 tons of fuel has been recovered and down-blended so that it is suitable for use in Oklo's Aurora plant). Oklo has partnerships with companies like Centrus and Hexium that it hopes will provide HALEU in the mid-term. Low-cost production isn't on the immediate horizon though, as Silex's first plant isn't expected to come online until around 2030. Longer-term, Oklo is relying on its Tennessee Advanced Fuel Center (recycle and fabricate domestically by leveraging used fuel inventories).

Oklo could also use plutonium-based feedstock and the government has significant reserves of plutonium. Up to 20 tons of plutonium will be made available in tranches, which could be made into roughly 180 tons of fuel for Aurora. This would be enough to supply the first 10-20 plants.

Atomic Alchemy has also been selected for the DoE's Reactor Pilot Program. Atomic Alchemy is developing a separate lab scale facility that will provide an early revenue stream through isotope sales. This reactor is expected to become operational in the middle of next year. It will also prove production pathways, validate supply chain and de-risk the larger VIPR facility. Longer term, Oklo is focused on securing an NRC license for a full-scale VIPR facility.

VIPR is a water-cooled and unpressurized reactor that uses LEU fuel to produce isotopes for health care, defense and industrial applications. While this probably isn't the focus of investors, it could be an important business in its own right given that isotopes are in shortage.

Neutron irradiation of a target in a reactor core can produce isotopes that require high neutron flux, such as:

  • Mo-99
  • I-131
  • Ir-192
  • Lu-177.

This approach to isotope production has relatively low marginal costs but the minimum viable scale is large due to upfront reactor costs. This means that reactors are generally used to produce high-volume isotopes at a global scale. Purity requirements and the market price of the relevant isotope are also important considerations.

Financial Analysis

Cash used in operating activities in 2025 expected to be in the $65-80 million USD range. This is low relatively to Oklo's $1.2 billion USD cash and marketable securities position. Cash burn will increase significantly as the scope of Oklo's operations increases in coming years.

I tend to think that access to capital remains an underappreciated problem for Oklo given its decision to build and operate its own facilities. The company will likely still need to raise billions of dollars, particularly if its timelines push out significantly. Oklo's 50 MWe plants have an estimated cost of $116 million USD at scale, although there is significant uncertainty in this estimate, which largely relates to civil works and fuel. Regardless, tens of billions of dollars will be needed for Oklo to fulfill its existing backlog. It is unclear what the company's path to free cash flow will look like but, assuming multi-year plant construction timelines and rapid growth, free cash flow isn't likely to occur until years into the future. Attractive financing options may be available once the feasibility of the business is established though.

Conclusion

Analysts are currently expecting revenue to really begin ramping in 2030 and generally remain bullish on the company due to the buildout of AI data centers. This situation has been aided by policy changes which have pulled forward initial deployment timelines.

Oklo is now moving into the deployment phase though, where technical difficulties and cost overruns are likely to occur. While it will take time, I believe this is where the marginal economics of SMRs and the capital requirements of Oklo's business will become a focal point for investors. As a result, I am negative on the stock's prospects over the next 1-2 years.

data.title: URTH: Further Dollar Weakness Provides A Narrow Path To Outperformance
data.description:
Introduction

Over the past decade, global diversification has been a drag on returns for U.S. investors. The iShares MSCI World ETF (URTH) has underperformed the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF by around 81% since 2015, delivering a still healthy total return of ~227%:

The silver lining is that 2025 has seen a small reversal of this trend, albeit far from enough to change the overall picture shown in the snippet above. Indeed we observe that URTH has outperformed VOO by roughly 3% in 2025, delivering a total return of ~22%:

Looking ahead to 2026, I see a narrow path for incremental URTH outperformance, largely dependent on USD weakness principally versus the JPY. As such, investors may view URTH as a tactical holding with around a 1-year horizon, with any USD weakness likely to play out in 2026 amid the finalization of Fed rate cuts.

That said, my overall rating for URTH is a Hold, justified by:

  • An elevated trailing P/E of ~26.27x despite 27.8% of assets being allocated to cheaper regions outside the United States.
  • A high allocation to information technology/communication services stocks, which have arguably seen a frontloading of returns in recent years.
  • An expense ratio of 0.24% which is likely to provide a further drag on returns relative to cheaper ETF alternatives.

In this article, I will expand on these points and discuss risks related to the URTH investment case.

ETF Overview

You can access all URTH details on the iShares website here. URTH provides a convenient way to gain exposure to global developed market equities (the benchmark is the MSCI World Index). This is a capitalization-weighted index, implying that while the ETF holds a total of 1,321 stocks, the top ten holdings account for an outsized 27.7% of ETF assets. The largest holdings are naturally American megacap companies such as NVIDIA (NVDA) (5.37% of ETF assets), Apple (AAPL) (4.97%), and Microsoft (MSFT) (4.06%).

As such, it is little surprise that the United States accounts for 72.2% of ETF assets, followed by Japan at 5.43%:

The high weight of U.S. megacaps also results in a natural tilt toward the information technology sector, which accounts for 27.75% of ETF assets, followed by financials at 16.51% and industrials at 11.01%:

Overall we observe that URTH is primarily a play on two themes - AI growth in information technology/communication services (combined weight of 36.55% of ETF assets) and a continued positive economic momentum in the global economy, with the combined weight of cyclical sectors such as financials, industrials, and consumer discretionary standing at 37.49%.

The ETF's expense ratio stands at 0.24%, making it an acceptable long-term holding for passive investors. Indeed when discussing ETFs with friends, I always advise them to go for ETFs with fees of 0.1-0.2% if they plan to hold them for a prolonged period of time.

Of course, if you are cost-conscious, you may split your URTH allocation in two: ~72% in the S&P 500-tracking VOO which offers an expense ratio of 0.03%, and ~28% in the iShares Core MSCI International Developed Markets ETF (IDEV) which has an expense ratio of 0.04%. My recent IDEV article is available here. In this way, you may potentially boost returns by around 0.2% annually.

Current Valuation

Given the ETF characteristics outlined above, namely a high weight of U.S. megacap stocks, URTH's current valuation is understandably quite elevated. Indeed the trailing P/E ratio stands at ~26.27x, or an earnings yield of about 3.81%.

This is only marginally better than the S&P 500's trailing P/E of 28.36x (earnings yield of 3.53%). Overall going into 2026, with URTH you are getting a roughly 0.25% higher earnings yield which is likely to be offset by slower GDP growth in the ~28% of ETF assets allocated outside the United States, as well as the ETF's marginally higher expense ratio compared with plain vanilla S&P 500 alternatives such as VOO.

Indeed looking at the IMF's World Economic Outlook, we observe that U.S. GDP growth is projected at about 2.1% in 2026, ahead of the 1.6% expansion for advanced economies:

All in all, from a purely valuation perspective, it is hard for me to see how URTH can sustainably outperform VOO, especially when we factor in the ETF's somewhat higher expense ratio.

URTH Rating

I believe that the case for further URTH outperformance next year will ultimately be decided by the U.S. dollar. If we see incremental dollar weakness, we are likely to have another year of URTH outperformance next year. Alternatively, if the dollar stabilizes around current levels, URTH is likely to perform largely in line with VOO.

My personal bias is for a marginally weaker U.S. dollar, driven largely by ongoing Fed rate cuts. This may come even as the Bank of Japan gradually tightens policy, potentially resulting in USD weakness versus the JPY, which accounts for 5.43% of URTH assets.

Even so, as discussed in the Current Valuation section, the underlying buy case for URTH, driven by attractive valuations, is not present at the moment. Hence my rating for URTH going into 2026 would be a Hold.

Risks

As discussed in the ETF Overview section, URTH principally represents a bet of two themes - AI growth and a strong performance for the global economy in 2026. Disappointment on either of those fronts will likely result in a weak 2026 for URTH.

While I am a bit skeptical of the AI theme given the frontloading of returns that occurred in recent years, I am generally optimistic for the global economy next year. Oil prices are low, and efforts are being made to address geopolitical conflicts around the world, albeit not always successfully. As such, in my opinion the key risk to watch in 2026 would be the valuation of AI companies.

For U.S. investors in particular, a risk worth highlighting is that a reversal of recent USD weakness may result in URTH underperforming VOO in 2026. Indeed roughly 28% of ETF assets are allocated outside the United States, with valuations dependent on the daily movement of currencies such as the JPY, GBP, and EUR relative to the USD.

Conclusion

The iShares MSCI World ETF has a poor 10-year track record relative to the S&P 500, with a small outperformance in 2025 not enough to change the overall trend in the prior nine years.

Looking ahead to 2026, I believe that the valuations the ETF's holdings trade at is not enough to justify a Buy rating, as marginally higher trailing earnings are likely to be offset by weaker GDP growth and a higher expense ratio.

Against this backdrop, I believe the only viable path to another year of higher URTH gains relative to VOO would be incremental USD weakness. As such, investors may want to view URTH as an attractive holding with a shorter-term time horizon, most probably the end of 2026. By that time, the Fed should be done with its policy normalization, likely resulting in a one-off drop in the USD principally against currencies which are seeing rate hikes, such as the JPY.

As this is a rather niche scenario to bet on, my overall rating on URTH is a Hold.

Key risks in the investment case stem from the ETF's two principal overweight positions in AI stocks and cyclical sectors. A reversal of USD weakness is a notable risk for U.S. investors as well.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Nebius: Unparalleled AI Infrastructure Moat
data.description:
Share price growth is never linear, even if fundamentals are strong. Therefore, I am not concerned much about the fact that Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS) dropped by around 17% since my previous bullish writeup. Since I am investing and not speculating/gambling, I prefer to pay more attention to fundamental analysis. After the new $3 billion AI infrastructure deal with Meta Platforms (META), the company has a total $20 billion backlog with hyperscalers. The management is focused on widening the company's technological moat, and it appears that they have everything they need to accomplish this goal.

I think that valuation uncertainty might play a role only over the short term in the case of Nebius because the company is riding the secular AI wave, meaning that it has the potential to maintain aggressive growth for longer. And with aggressive long-term growth, multiples will compress inevitably. My take is that NBIS remains a Strong Buy opportunity.

Fundamental analysis

The last time I covered Nebius was late September; therefore, the $3 billion AI infrastructure deal with Meta is a new development for my thesis. Taking into account that Nebius inked a $17.4 billion AI infrastructure deal with Microsoft (MSFT) a couple of months earlier, the company's total backlog with hyperscaler exposure is $20+ billion. A large positive factor for a $23 billion worth company, which provides long-term revenue visibility of the company.

Moreover, it is not just about the numbers but about the quality as well. Having deals with Microsoft and AI validates Nebius's technological edge and its reputation as a highly reliable AI infrastructure partner. A solid foundation for Nebius to capitalize on the AI infrastructure boom with the forecasted 27% industry CAGR for the next decade.

With such a bullish setup with Microsoft and Meta in Nebius's corner, I am highly convinced that the company's soaring revenue over the last few quarters is just the tip of the iceberg. Soaring AI infrastructure demand is also confirmed by the management's aggressive CapEx guidance increase for FY2025, from $2 billion to $5 billion. With almost $5 billion in cash and just $800 million in short-term liabilities, Nebius is well-equipped financially to invest in further revenue debottlenecking. Moreover, with publicly announced contracts with giants like Microsoft and Meta, the company's potential to raise more finance has also been fortified.

Of course, AI infrastructure is 'rocket science,' as it requires deep expertise and stellar execution. Nvidia's GPUs are not cheap, meaning that building a mega-scale data center requires surgery-like precision. And I expect execution to be the area where Nebius excels compared to all other AI infrastructure players of comparable size because the company employs around 1,000 hardware and software engineers who already have practical experience building a massive cloud and AI infrastructure for Yandex, one of the most (if not the most) technologically advanced companies in Europe. Therefore, Nebius is a young startup only by form but not by substance.

Of course, we all know that Google dominates the global search engine industry, and all its closest competitors command quite small market shares. However, the fact that Yandex's global search engine market share is ahead of America's Yahoo and China's Baidu speaks volumes about the quality of this company's engineers. Therefore, Nebius's engineering team is among the strongest in the western world and probably trails only Amazon's (AMZN) AWS, Microsoft, and Google (GOOG). Having such a team is certainly a big competitive advantage for the company, which is arguably impossible to replicate if you are not one of the aforementioned U.S. cloud behemoths.

Having a strong engineering team that consists of both hardware and software professionals is a big competitive advantage because it positions the company to build a full-stack AI infrastructure company and not just be a “GPU rental service.” Implementing a 'one-stop-shop' approach has stronger potential to drive growth as it creates cross-sell opportunities, which in turn increases switching costs for customers. If we return to Yandex once again, Arkady Volozh and his team have built a massive ecosystem that includes not only search but also a suite of office applications, maps, taxis, and food/grocery delivery. Therefore, besides strong potential to execute via the hands of these 1,000 engineers, Nebius also does not lack visionary talent. Finally, there is another differentiator that might be overlooked, but I consider very important.

During the recent UBS Global Technology and AI Conference, the management shared one interesting detail: their support team consists of AI engineers, which is a unique differentiator that significantly improves customer experience, as AI engineers have sufficient expertise to solve even complicated issues by themselves. I think that this “under-the-radar” differentiator streamlines the whole process compared to having just general specialists in the support team that have limited expertise (if any) to solve inquiries.

Due to all these positive catalysts, it is no wonder that NBIS remains the SA Quant darling with a 4.94 "Strong Buy" rating. Besides having a high Quant grade, there is big optimism from Wall Street analysts as well. The average target price is $151, implying a massive 60% upside potential.

Mitigating factors

While mentioning all the positive factors, it is also important to balance out and speak about risks and uncertainties as well. The company's exponential revenue growth and its strong ability to differentiate are all good. However, it is also fair to emphasize that Nebius is still not profitable since its EPS is negative, like the TTM operating cash flow. The level of uncertainty is also amplified by the fact that there is no clear trend of the bottom-line loss narrowing over the next several quarters, as the EPS is expected to remain quite volatile at least by the end of FY2026.

All these uncertainties around the EPS and continued operating cash burn also raise questions about the company's valuation. The uncertainty around valuation was not a big problem during the AI mania phase, but as the overall "AI bubble" narrative is becoming louder, it might become a problem for investors of NBIS. What I mean is that if there is a broader market selloff triggered by an "AI bubble" panic, stocks of companies that are still not profitable, like Nebius, are poised to suffer a severe selloff.

Therefore, readers should keep separate the company and the stock. Nebius is a company that has great chances to succeed over the long term. However, during a broader market's panic, share price and fundamentals might move completely disconnected from each other. For example, Amazon's share price decreased by more than ten times during the "dot-com" bubble outburst while the business continued expanding rapidly. I am not saying that the same will definitely happen to NBIS, but it is one of the potential scenarios if there is really an "AI bubble.

Conclusion

I think that the recent dip presents a compelling buying opportunity if you are ready to "buy and hold" NBIS for decades. I think that Nebius has great potential to become one of the most important companies in the AI era, given its strong technological moat and great visionary talent.

data.title: SPEU: A Robust Play On European Equities And Dollar Weakness
data.description:
Thesis

The SPDR Portfolio Europe ETF (SPEU) is an equities exchange-traded fund. The name comes from State Street and has been in the market for more than a decade. With a deteriorating fiscal picture and a potentially weaker dollar going forward, we are going to have a close look at SPEU and its risk drivers and articulate our view on the ETF from a weaker dollar perspective.

What does this fund do?

Let us start with basic fund information for SPEU by looking at its objective:

The SPDR® Portfolio Europe ETF seeks to provide investment results that, before fees and expenses, correspond generally to the total return performance of the STOXX® Europe Total Market Index (the “Index”)

The ETF is a passive name, following the STOXX Europe Total Market Index. The fund charges just 0.07% for its services, in line with large passive ETFs. If we look at the followed Index and its objective, we get the following:

The STOXX Europe Total Market Index represents the Western Europe region as a whole. With a variable number of components, it covers approximately 95 percent of the free float market capitalization across 17 European countries: Austria, Belgium, Poland, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Ireland, Italy, Luxembourg, the Netherlands, Norway, Portugal, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland and the United Kingdom.

SPEU is therefore an ETF that offers investors exposure to European equities. Let us look closer at the fund components and analytics.

Fund components

The fund is granular, with over 1,800 individual holdings, and is market weighted, with the top five names as follows:

  • ASML Holdings N.V. (ASML): 2.8%
  • Roche Holdings AG (RHHBY): 1.8%
  • AstraZeneca PLC (AZN): 1.85%
  • HSBC Holdings plc (HSBC): 1.7%
  • Novartis AG (NVS): 1.6%

From a geographical standpoint, the top countries represented in the ETF are:

  • United Kingdom: 22.3%
  • France: 15.6%
  • Germany: 14%
  • Switzerland: 14%
  • Netherlands: 6.6%
  • Sweden: 6.2%
  • Spain: 5.4%
  • Italy: 5.3%
  • Denmark: 3%
  • Belgium: 1.8%
  • Poland: 1%

The top three names account for roughly 50% of the fund currently. To note that one of the outperformers in 2025, namely Poland, is only 1% of the fund.

From a sectoral standpoint, we have the following parsing:

  • Financials: 24%
  • Industrials: 20%
  • Health Care: 12%
  • Consumer discretionary: 9%
  • Consumer staples: 8.5%
  • IT: 7.3%
  • Materials: 5.4%
  • Energy: 4%
  • Utilities: 4%
  • Communication services: 3.5%

The fund falls in the 'Large Cap' category when looking at the Morningstar classification, meaning that a large proportion of the fund holdings are large capitalization equities. The portfolio valuation metrics are as follows:

  • P/E ratio: 16x
  • P/B ratio: 2.1x

Historic performance and alternatives

The fund has had an extraordinary performance this year, but it has not always been so:

  • 2018: -13.7%
  • 2019: +26.1%
  • 2020: +6.3%
  • 2021: +16.1%
  • 2022: -15.9%
  • 2023: +19.8%
  • 2024: +1.9%
  • YTD: +33.4%

In fact, this year has seen the largest gain for the name on an annual basis for a decade. The fund was down when rates went up in 2018 and in 2022. Long term, the ETF does deliver, with a 10-year annualized total return at 8.5%.

The ETF represents the State Street offering for European equities, but the name does have competitors:

We can see the tight correlation and similar total returns when looking at the iShares Core MSCI Europe ETF (IEUR), the Vanguard FTSE Developed Markets Index Fund (VEA), and SPEU.

From a risk analytic perspective, the name comes with a 13.2% standard deviation and a 0.9 Sharpe ratio. During run-of-the-mill market pullbacks, the fund sees -10% drawdowns (as in April 2025); during mild recessions or severe pullbacks, the fund sees -20% drawdowns (January 2016, December 2018), while severe corrections see -30% drawdowns (Covid, October 2022).

The fund has a high correlation to the S&P 500 index but in the past decade has produced weaker annual returns, with 2025 being the first year when SPEU is on track to generate a higher net return when compared to the American index.

Dollar impact and lack of hedging

One of the most appealing aspects for SPEU is the lack of currency hedging. The dollar has been under pressure in 2025 on the back of a spiraling fiscal deficit and inclination to lower rates by the Federal Reserve. The Capital Group articulates that as well in the provided link, with the following excerpt from their piece:

A combination of loose US fiscal and monetary policy implies negative US long-term real yields and a weaker US dollar.

Now a fund like SPEU is ideal for such conditions since it provides exposure to equities, while at the same time benefiting from a weaker dollar:

The above graph contains three securities: SPEU, a hedged European equities fund via the WisdomTree Europe Hedged Equity Fund (HEDJ), and the Invesco CurrencyShares Euro Trust (FXE). FXE is basically a fund that tracks the Euro/USD currency pair, and the name is up +13% this year, meaning the dollar has depreciated by 13% versus the euro.

While HEDJ and SPEU do not have the exact same portfolios, do notice how the difference in total returns is quite close to the dollar depreciation. Basically, SPEU holders have gotten a bump in dollar terms from their unhedged European stocks when compared to a hedged fund like HEDJ.

While the P/E for European equities via this fund is low at 16x versus more than 23x for the S&P 500, do note that generally European equities have traded with more depressed P/E levels versus U.S. peers. Currently the Europe valuations are also towards the top of the range, but we could see an expansion in multiples in Europe.

Given the high fiscal deficit and weak dollar thematic, we like SPEU here, especially when compared to the stretched S&P 500. We favor a rotation into the name from stretched U.S. equities.

Conclusion

SPEU is an equities ETF. The name follows the STOXX Europe Total Market Index and provides for exposure to European equities without a hedge. A weaker dollar has boosted SPEU this year by almost 10%, while the name has recorded its best performance in the past decade at +33% so far. The holdings have a low portfolio P/E of only 16x, although historically European equities have traded with lower multiples. We like the name going into 2026 via the potential for multiple expansions and further gains from dollar weakness.
